data.title: Diebold Nixdorf targets $255M–$270M free cash flow in 2026 as operating model strengthens
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Diebold Nixdorf (DBD) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Octavio Marquez, President and CEO, highlighted 2025 as a defining year, stating Diebold Nixdorf “delivered on our commitments and most importantly, demonstrated that we are now operating a sustainable free cash flow generator with significantly more stable and predictable financial profile.” He pointed to a record $239 million in free cash flow, a doubling from the prior year, and emphasized expanded adjusted EBITDA to $485 million. Marquez noted, “We are delivering on what we said we would do quarter after quarter. This consistency is increasing predictability in our model and strengthening confidence in our long-term outlook.”
  • Marquez described growth in both banking and retail, with banking expanding into broader branch and transaction ecosystems and retail achieving three consecutive quarters of revenue growth, particularly in North America. He cited the successful pilot and transition to live store implementations of SmartVision AI solutions with a major U.S. retailer as a significant milestone.
  • The CEO underscored a record fifth consecutive quarter of positive free cash flow and two credit rating upgrades during the year. He affirmed that returning capital to shareholders remains central, referencing net leverage around 1x and ongoing share repurchases.
  • Thomas Timko, Executive VP & CFO, stated: “Q4 revenue was $1.1 billion, up 12% year-over-year and 17% sequentially, driven by growth in both product and service. In banking, high-capacity fit-for-purpose ATMs and strong performance in Europe drove results. In retail, strong point-of-sale and self-checkout performance globally drove revenue increases.”
  • Timko explained, “Q4 adjusted EBITDA reached $164 million, up 46% year-over-year with 350 basis points of margin expansion. Sequentially, we delivered 35% growth and drove an additional 200 basis points of margin improvement, bringing EBITDA margins to 14.9%.”

Outlook

  • The company introduced 2026 guidance with revenue projected between $3.86 billion and $3.94 billion, adjusted EBITDA in the range of $510 million to $535 million, and free cash flow forecast at $255 million to $270 million. Adjusted EPS guidance for 2026 is $5.25 to $5.75, assuming a 35% to 40% effective tax rate. Management pointed out, “At the midpoint, this guidance reflects approximately 22% year-over-year growth by a comparable basis when excluding certain noncash nonoperational tax benefits in 2025.”
  • The company expects product margins to remain at current high levels and service gross margin to increase up to 50 basis points year-over-year. Free cash flow is expected to remain positive in every quarter of 2026.
  • Marquez stated the company is “entering the year with momentum, a fortress balance sheet and a clear focus on delivering another year of profitable growth and cash generation.”

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue was $1.1 billion, with total gross margin expanding to 27.1%. Operating profit for the quarter reached $129 million, while Q4 adjusted EBITDA totaled $164 million. Adjusted EPS for Q4 was $3.02 and for the full year reached $5.59, including $1.08 of noncash, nonoperational favorable items.
  • For the year, the company reported $239 million in free cash flow, more than doubling the previous year’s result of $109 million. Over $128 million was returned to shareholders via repurchases, representing around 6% of the company at an average share price of $55.47.
  • Banking segment Q4 revenue grew 11% year-over-year, with product revenue up 20% and service revenue up 5%. Retail Q4 revenue increased 12% year-over-year to over $300 million, driven by point-of-sale and self-checkout. Retail service revenue rose 8% year-over-year in Q4.
  • The company ended 2025 with over $700 million of liquidity, including $416 million in cash and an undrawn $310 million revolver, and a net debt leverage ratio at 1.1x.

Q&A

  • Matt Summerville, D.A. Davidson: Questioned first-half versus second-half revenue cadence and Q1 expectations. Marquez answered that revenue cadence should mirror 2025, with “revenues to flow very similar to 2025 with Q1 being approximately 22% of our total revenue for the year.”
  • Summerville: Asked about the impact of service investments. Marquez responded that the North America service rollout is mostly complete and that Q1 will see a slight decrease in service margins, followed by improvement from Q2 as investments yield returns, with margins expected to grow up to 50 basis points.
  • Summerville: Sought clarification on U.S. retail logo wins. Timko explained that wins were spread across grocery, pharmacy, and QSR, and the U.S. retail business is expected to grow double digits, with AI playing an important role.
  • Justin Ages, CJS Securities: Asked about further potential for improving days sales outstanding (DSO) and days inventory outstanding (DIO). Marquez indicated there is additional opportunity, expecting another 4 to 5 days improvement in DSO.
  • Ages: Inquired about capital allocation between share repurchases, tuck-ins, and refinancing the $950 million note. Marquez emphasized that share repurchases remain the priority, but the company maintains flexibility for tuck-ins, especially in services.
  • Antoine Legault, Wedbush: Asked about the opportunity ahead for banking recyclers. Timko described penetration as a “continuous cycle,” expecting to maintain shipment volume and offset margin changes in Asia through Lean initiatives.
  • Legault: Requested factors influencing EPS guidance. Marquez cited continued share buybacks and post-tax operating profit as key drivers.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was generally positive, with questions reflecting interest in growth cadence, capital allocation, and operational improvements, but also seeking clarity on margin sustainability and investment priorities.
  • Management’s tone was confident and upbeat, frequently referencing strong execution, consistent performance, and momentum into 2026. Marquez used phrases like “entering the year with momentum” and emphasized that the company’s cash generation is “underappreciated.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management continue to display confidence, with analysts showing increased focus on capital returns and operational leverage.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from reaffirmation at the high end of ranges in Q3 to establishing higher targets for 2026, including revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and free cash flow.
  • Strategic focus has evolved from stabilizing the foundation to emphasizing growth, margin expansion, and capital allocation, particularly share repurchases.
  • Analysts remained focused on service margin recovery, retail growth, and capital allocation, while management highlighted continued operational improvements, Lean initiatives, and global expansion.
  • Financial results demonstrated acceleration in revenue, EBITDA, and free cash flow, while operating leverage and margin expansion were more prominent this quarter.
  • Management’s confidence and messaging around predictability, cash generation, and shareholder returns have strengthened compared to the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing investments in service infrastructure and technician hiring, which may temporarily impact service margins in Q1 2026 before improvement is realized.
  • There is a note on regional variability, particularly in Asia and Latin America, which may affect growth and margins, though the company expects to offset these through its Lean initiatives and diversified business model.
  • Analysts probed the limits of further working capital improvements and the balance between buybacks and strategic acquisitions, reflecting concerns about sustainable cash generation and capital allocation discipline.

Final Takeaway

Diebold Nixdorf’s management positions the company as having completed a transformational year, highlighting improved financial stability, record free cash flow, and robust growth across core banking and retail businesses. Entering 2026, the outlook features higher revenue and profit targets, continued margin expansion, and a strong commitment to capital returns, with management reiterating confidence in the durability of its operating model and ongoing ability to deliver shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Karyopharm signals $130M–$150M revenue target for 2026 with pivotal myelofibrosis data readout ahead
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Karyopharm Therapeutics Inc. (KPTI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Richard Paulson, President and CEO, highlighted Karyopharm entering a “defining phase, marked by important late-stage clinical milestones, continued disciplined execution and the opportunity to meaningfully expand the impact and scale of our oncology franchise.” He indicated selinexor’s “durable commercial foundation in multiple myeloma” and identified myelofibrosis as the most significant near-term value driver, with endometrial cancer as a subsequent expansion opportunity.
  • Paulson stated, “We remain on track to share top line data from our Phase III SENTRY trial in March,” positioning this as a key milestone. He added, “We continue to manage the business with discipline. As previously disclosed, our cash runway extends into the second quarter, which aligns with key near-term clinical milestones.”
  • Reshma Rangwala, Executive VP, Chief Medical Officer & Head of Research, emphasized: “I’m incredibly excited by the near-term opportunity to read out two Phase III trials that could establish new standards of care in 2 areas of high unmet need.” She detailed selinexor’s mechanism and potential in myelofibrosis, referencing “a substantial body of preclinical, nonclinical translational and clinical efficacy as well as safety data sets.”
  • Sohanya Cheng, Chief Commercial Officer, reported, “XPOVIO net product revenue grew to $32.1 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 and $114.9 million for full year 2025. We expect to continue to deliver revenue growth this year and are guiding towards $115 million to $130 million of XPOVIO net product revenue in 2026.”
  • Lori Macomber, CFO, stated, “Total revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $34.1 million, an increase of 11.8% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024. For the year, total revenue was $146.1 million, a slight increase from 2024.” She also noted, “Our loss from operations improved by approximately 43% in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.”

Outlook

  • Macomber provided 2026 financial guidance: “Total revenue of $130 million to $150 million, consisting of U.S. XPOVIO net product revenue and license royalty and milestone revenue expected to be earned from our partners, primarily Menarini and Antengene. U.S. XPOVIO net product revenue to be in the range of $115 million to $130 million. R&D and SG&A expenses to be in the range of $230 million to $245 million.”
  • Paulson described the company as “well positioned as we approach pivotal data that will inform the next phase of the company,” and reaffirmed commitment to “managing the business with a clear focus on near-term value-creating milestones.”
  • There was no explicit change to guidance language compared to the previous quarter, but the focus is now squarely on the upcoming March data readout for myelofibrosis.

Financial Results

  • XPOVIO net product revenue was $32.1 million in Q4 2025 and $114.9 million for the full year.
  • Total revenue for Q4 2025 was $34.1 million, and $146.1 million for the year.
  • Research and development expenses for Q4 were $27.7 million, down 17% year-over-year; full year R&D expenses were $125.6 million, down 12%.
  • Selling, general and administrative expenses for Q4 were $22.8 million, down 16% year-over-year; full year SG&A was $105.2 million, down 9%.
  • Net loss was $102.2 million or $5.71 per share in Q4, and $196 million or $17.93 per diluted share for the full year, with Macomber explaining, “More than half of the full year loss was driven by below-the-line items, including the loss on extinguishment of debt and interest expense, which are largely noncash in nature.”
  • Cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and investments at year-end were $64.1 million.

Q&A

  • Colleen Hanley, Robert W. Baird & Co.: Asked about differences between 40 mg and 60 mg dosing in Phase I. Rangwala responded, “When you look at the efficacy and safety, there's a clear benefit risk in favor of the 60-milligram cohort as compared to the 40 mg, largely due to the fact that efficacy, both from an SVR as well as TSS was maximized in the 60-milligram group as compared to the 40-milligram group.”
  • Hanley asked about Novartis’s moves in myelofibrosis. Paulson answered, “Given our top line readout data is going to happen in March, we would look to be filing rapidly with our data, obviously engaging in both the U.S. and globally.”
  • Ioannis Souroutzidis, Cantor Fitzgerald: Inquired about blinded safety data and dose modifications. Rangwala replied, “No, we haven't updated anything beyond what we've publicly disclosed in the past,” and added that dose modifications for selinexor and ruxolitinib were per protocol and consistent with label guidance.
  • Brian Abrahams, RBC Capital Markets: Questioned patient population and efficacy expectations. Rangwala stated, “Yes, this potentially could be a less sick patient population. With that said, though, I don't think it's going to have any impact on the efficacy.”
  • Jonathan Chang, Leerink Partners: Asked about confidence in hitting the TSS endpoint. Rangwala cited “multiple different aspects that give us confidence,” including strong Phase I data, cytokine reductions, and the FDA’s acceptance of the more sensitive absolute TSS endpoint.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were optimistic but focused on clarifying trial details, dosing rationale, and competitive positioning. Their tone was engaged and constructive, with no overt skepticism or negativity.
  • Management maintained a confident and energized tone throughout, particularly regarding near-term clinical milestones and the commercial outlook. Paulson described the organization as “energized and well positioned to deliver on this opportunity.”
  • The tone from both analysts and management is largely consistent with the previous quarter, but the urgency and anticipation have increased as pivotal data approaches.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The strategic focus has shifted from trial enrollment and financial restructuring to imminent clinical data readouts and commercial execution for myelofibrosis.
  • Financial discipline and cost control remain a theme, with further reductions in R&D and SG&A expenses.
  • Guidance for 2026 is in line with prior outlook, but now incorporates the anticipated impact of upcoming clinical milestones.
  • Analysts’ questions have become more technically focused on trial design, data interpretation, and competitive positioning versus prior quarter’s broader commercial strategy queries.
  • Management’s confidence and anticipation are more pronounced as pivotal results near, compared to the more methodical tone in the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Macomber reminded that cash runway “extends into the second quarter, which aligns with key near-term clinical milestones,” highlighting the need for additional financing depending on trial outcomes.
  • Paulson acknowledged “actively evaluating a range of financing and strategic options to maintain flexibility and align capital decisions with value creation.”
  • The end of R&D reimbursement from Menarini in 2025 was stated, impacting future non-product revenue streams.
  • No new safety or trial discontinuation concerns were raised; management reiterated protocol adherence and confidence in trial design.

Final Takeaway

Karyopharm management underscores a pivotal inflection point in 2026, with the Phase III SENTRY trial in myelofibrosis set for a March data readout, and top line results from the XPORT-EC-042 endometrial cancer trial expected mid-year. The company reaffirms disciplined cost management and financial guidance of $130 million to $150 million in total revenue for 2026, highlighting a durable commercial foundation and readiness for potential expansion pending positive trial outcomes. Management remains focused on executing near-term value-creating milestones and maintaining operational flexibility as Karyopharm advances its late-stage oncology pipeline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold and silver plunge in sudden selloff as stock market rout triggers algo trading
data.description:
Gold and silver futures plummeted Thursday as strong U.S. labor data dimmed hopes of near-term Federal Reserve rate cuts, with algorithmic traders apparently exacerbating the sudden drop.

The plunge in precious metals came as the stock market fell sharply, as investors worried the artificial intelligence buildout might disrupt the business models of whole industries and hurt some companies' future earnings, triggering a rout across risk assets.

The swift drop in the metals - which did not have an obvious catalyst - likely was intensified by selling from commodity trading advisors using computer models to bet on price moves, Bloomberg strategist Michael Ball said.

"Metals are overall getting hit hard in a sudden air-pocket kind of move lower that feels more like systematic strategy selling, the kind of momentum-driven de-risking you often see from the CTA community when levels give way," Ball wrote.

Stop-selling was blamed by Fawad Razaqzada, market analyst at City Index and Forex.com.

"Due to previous heightened volatility, a lot of people would have placed their stops either below $5,000 or above the $5,100 level just to preserve their stop positions," Razaqzada said in a note. "Because of the downward move, those stops have been triggered below the $5,000 level, and that caused a cascading-like effect, causing prices to slump in a short period of time."

"The market has now cleared out a large pocket of downside liquidity, and the next move will depend on how price behaves around key technical levels," Razaqzada added.

Gold and silver prices fell initially after strong U.S. jobs data tempered near-term expectations for fresh interest rate cuts, as non-farm payrolls rose by 130K jobs in January following a downwardly revised 48K increase in December, the unemployment rate edged down to 4.3%, and initial jobless claims fell to 227K in the week ended February 7.

Investors will watch U.S. inflation data due on Friday for more cues on the Fed's monetary policy path.

Finally, some of the day's selloff in the metals likely came from typical profit-taking, as the most recent blistering rally was driven partly by speculative buying.

"For gold and silver, a great deal of trading remains sentiment and momentum driven. On days like these, they will struggle," Saxo Bank's Ole Hansen said in a note.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for February delivery closed -2.9% to $4,923.70/oz, and front-month Comex February silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished -9.8% to $75.546/oz, in the largest one-day decline for both metals since January 30.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Avient outlines $555M–$585M adjusted EBITDA target for 2026 while expanding innovation and debt reduction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Avient Corporation (AVNT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Ashish Khandpur, President, CEO & Chairman of the Board, highlighted "strong execution by our teams with favorable mix and management's tight cost control led to 80 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion and a strong 14% adjusted EPS growth for the fourth quarter." He noted that adjusted EBITDA margins expanded year-over-year in each quarter of 2025, and organic sales in Q4 were down 0.8% but grew 1.9% as reported due to favorable foreign exchange impact.
  • Khandpur pointed to double-digit business growth in defense, health care, and telecom, with packaging demand "improved modestly, growing sales low single digits in the fourth quarter compared to being down low single digits in the third quarter." He said, "adjusted EBITDA finished at $545 million for 2025 with 3.5% year-over-year growth as reported. Adjusted EPS grew 6%, helped by lower interest expense and favorable foreign currencies."
  • Khandpur emphasized innovation as a strategic driver, citing new non-PFAS products for catheters and packaging, along with process innovation in Dyneema fiber, designed to unlock new capacity to meet anticipated defense growth. "We plan to deploy incremental capital over the next 2 years to further expand capacity and support the growth we foresee from our Dyneema-based businesses."
  • Khandpur reflected on the company's evolved strategy since 2024, prioritizing organic growth, targeted M&A, profitability, and elimination of structural complexity. "Over the last 2 years, we have grown adjusted earnings per share by about 20% and expanded adjusted EBITDA margins by 70 basis points."
  • He stated, "Our strong free cash flow generation and disciplined capital deployment also allowed us to reduce debt, bringing net leverage down from 3.1x in 2023 to 2.6x in 2025."
  • Jamie Beggs, Senior VP & CFO, explained, "Continued strength in health care and improving packaging demand was not enough to offset demand conditions in consumer, industrial and building and construction, which led to a 3% decline in organic sales for the segment during the quarter. EBITDA margins declined 10 basis points as productivity initiatives helped mitigate the impact of inflation and reduced demand."
  • Beggs added, "Specialty Engineered Materials organic sales increased 3% as strong growth in defense, health care and telecommunications more than offset lower sales in energy, industrial and building and construction end markets." She highlighted 10% EBITDA growth in this segment for Q4.

Outlook

  • Beggs stated, "Our full year guidance reflects a balance between encouraging demand trends across our portfolio and continued macro uncertainty and volatility." She set full year 2026 guidance for adjusted EBITDA of $555 million to $585 million and adjusted EPS of $2.93 to $3.17. First quarter adjusted EPS outlook is $0.81.
  • She also outlined anticipated free cash flow of $200 million to $220 million for 2026, with capital expenditures of $140 million, primarily to support growth in the defense business. "Productivity will again play a role in supporting earnings growth and margin expansion in 2026."

Financial Results

  • Khandpur reported $545 million in adjusted EBITDA for 2025, 3.5% year-over-year growth, and record-high adjusted EBITDA margins of 16.7%. Adjusted EPS increased 6% for the year, and free cash flow reached $195 million. The company reduced outstanding debt by $150 million, ending the year with a net leverage ratio of 2.6x.
  • Beggs stated, "CAI organic sales declined 2%. Steady growth in health care throughout 2025 helped offset softer demand in consumer, industrial, transportation and building and construction. Packaging remained relatively resilient, ending the year flat versus 2024."
  • Specialty Engineered Materials organic sales grew 2%, with EBITDA margins declining 40 basis points due to planned maintenance and strategic investments.

Q&A

  • Michael Sison, Wells Fargo: Are you seeing green shoots in consumer, industrial, transportation, and construction areas? What improvement is needed to hit midpoint guidance? Khandpur: Part of the improvement is expected in Q1 for consumer and packaging in the U.S. "We are seeing better conditions than we were seeing before on the packaging side. As you saw that in Q4 itself, packaging was up 1% in the United States." On the guidance midpoint, "that assumes that there is a modest growth in all of there, probably low single-digit kind of growth and packaging also a little bit more modest growth."
  • Sison: How much growth in 2026 from innovation? Khandpur: Patent filings doubled over two years to 50-plus. "From a financial perspective, if you take 2025, our growth vectors grew high single digits."
  • Daniel Rizzo, Jefferies: Outlook for transportation? Khandpur: "Transportation for us... was overall down minus 1% for the year. In United States, the market was down minus 1% for the year, but we were down minus 5%." Q1 likely softer in Asia, with full year projection as "flattish to low single digit."
  • Rizzo: Should we expect more internal growth or M&A? Khandpur: "Probably not this year, maybe something after that when we also have a better balance sheet situation."
  • Frank Mitsch, Fermium: Debt paydown vs. buybacks? Khandpur: "In the near term, you can expect us to keep paying the debt versus buyback."
  • Unknown Analyst, KeyBanc: Productivity gains and new actions? Beggs: "We accomplished a little over $40 million of net productivity from last year. We expect about half of that to basically continue on into 2026."
  • Kristen Owen, Oppenheimer: Progress toward 20% EBITDA margin? Beggs: "Our goal is to get above 20% EBITDA margins... the last couple of years has been more focused on the mix dynamic and the productivity dynamic."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on macro improvement questions, innovation, and capital allocation, frequently pressing for specifics on market recovery and margin expansion. The sentiment was neutral to slightly positive, with cautious optimism.
  • Management maintained a confident yet cautious outlook, frequently referencing "cautiously optimistic" and noting both improvements and ongoing volatility. During Q&A, management provided detailed, data-driven responses, often reiterating the need for productivity and innovation.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from defensive and contingency-focused to more optimistic, supported by recent margin and innovation gains. Analysts appeared more constructive and less skeptical than in the previous quarter, with fewer questions on downside risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more specific and optimistic, projecting adjusted EBITDA growth to $555 million–$585 million and adjusted EPS of $2.93–$3.17, compared to the prior quarter’s guidance range of $540 million–$550 million EBITDA and $2.77–$2.87 EPS.
  • Strategic focus continues on innovation and organic growth, with clear examples of recent product launches and process advancements. The previous quarter’s emphasis on contingency planning has given way to detailed capital deployment and capacity expansion plans.
  • Analysts’ focus has shifted from inventory and demand risks to questions about margin progression and innovation-driven growth.
  • Management’s confidence has grown, as evidenced by repeated references to record margin performance, debt reduction, and innovation pipeline progress.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing macro volatility, trade policy uncertainties, and geopolitical risks as potential headwinds for 2026.
  • Beggs described $30 million net inflation as a baseline challenge, with the company prepared to scale productivity initiatives based on demand. The CFO noted, "uncertainty remains with evolving global trade, labor market, GDP growth rates and foreign currency fluctuations."
  • Analysts questioned improvements in end markets and the sustainability of innovation-driven growth, but management reiterated preparedness with cost and productivity levers if demand weakens.

Final Takeaway

Avient’s leadership reported record margins and consistent earnings growth for 2025, supported by innovation, disciplined capital deployment, and targeted productivity initiatives. The company projects another year of earnings expansion in 2026, with adjusted EBITDA guidance of $555 million to $585 million and a focus on continued debt reduction, capacity investments in defense, and scaling growth vectors through new differentiated products. Management maintains cautious optimism amid lingering macro uncertainties, emphasizing readiness to drive profitability and margin expansion regardless of market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: PTC Therapeutics drops as it pulls Translarna NDA resubmission
data.description:
• PTC Therapeutics (PTCT) is down ~5% in after-hours trading Thursday after announcing it has withdrawn its NDA resubmission of Translarna (ataluren) for nonsense mutation Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
  • The company made the decision based on US FDA feedback on its application review.
  • CEO Matthew Klein said that the data included in the application is "unlikely to meet the Agency's threshold of substantial evidence of effectiveness to support approval."
  • The drug is approved in the European Union.

data.title: Concentrix prices $600 million senior notes offering
data.description:
• Concentrix (CNXC) on Thursday said it has priced a public offering of $600 million aggregate principal amount of 6.500% Senior Notes due 2029.
  • The company expects to use the net proceeds from the offering, together with other available funds, as necessary, to redeem or otherwise repay at or prior to maturity all or a portion of its 6.650% Senior Notes due August 2, 2026, of which $800 million aggregate principal amount is outstanding as of the date hereof, and pay related fees and expenses.
  • The offering is expected to close on February 24, 2026.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: DexCom Q4 results beat on both lines; 2026 revenue guidance includes consensus
data.description:
• DexCom's (DXCM) Q4 2025 financial results beat on both lines and the company reiterated its 2026 guidance range, which includes the consensus.
  • The maker of continuous glucose monitors sees revenue this year of $5.16B-$5.25B. Consensus is $5.23B.
  • Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $0.68 compares to $0.45 in the year-ago period. 
  • DexCom was helped in the quarter by a 13% year-over-year revenue increase to $1.26B. US revenue rose by 11%, while international revenue grew 18%.
  • The company ended 2025 with cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $2B compared to $2.58B on Dec. 31, 2024.
